questions.text: Which one of the following is the best condition for performing maximum work by any thermodynamic system?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: In an adiabatic process, there is no heat exchange with the surroundings. While it can lead to changes in internal energy, it is not the best condition for maximizing work output.
In an isobaric process, pressure remains constant, allowing for significant work to be done as the volume changes. This is typically not the condition for maximum work in thermodynamic cycles.
An isochoric process involves no change in volume, meaning no work is done by the system, as work is a product of pressure change and volume change.

In an isothermal process, the temperature remains constant. This condition is ideal for performing maximum work, especially in reversible processes, as it allows the system to exchange heat with the surroundings efficiently while performing work.

questions.explanation: The isothermal condition is the best for performing maximum work, particularly in reversible processes, because the system can exchange heat with the surroundings while maintaining a constant temperature. This allows for efficient energy transfer and work performance. In contrast, adiabatic processes involve no heat exchange, which limits the work potential; isobaric processes maintain constant pressure, which does not necessarily maximize work; and isochoric processes do no work since the volume does not change.

questions.text: In an isothermal condition of any thermodynamic system, the change in internal energy
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. If the temperature doesn’t change, there’s no increase in internal energy. So it doesn’t become maximum.
This option is incorrect. Same logic — there’s no decrease in internal energy. It doesn’t become minimum either.
This option is correct. The change in internal energy is zero because the temperature is constant. This applies especially to ideal gases.

This option is also technically correct, because if the change is zero, that means the internal energy remains constant throughout the process. However, while C (becomes zero) and D (remains constant) might seem similar, C specifically answers the question about the change in internal energy.

questions.explanation: In an isothermal process change in energy ΔU becomes zero.

questions.text: In which of the following conditions, does the thermodynamic system DOES NOT perform any work?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. In an adiabatic process, no heat is exchanged with the surroundings, but work can still be done as the system expands or contracts.
This option is correct. In an isochoric process, the volume remains constant. Since work is defined as W=PΔV, where ΔV=0 means no work is performed. Therefore, no work is done in this process.
This option is incorrect. In an isobaric process, the pressure remains constant, and work is performed as the volume changes. The work done can be calculated using the formula W=PΔV.
This option is incorrect. In an isothermal process, the temperature remains constant, and work can be done as the system expands or contracts, even if the internal energy does not change.

questions.explanation: In an isochoric process, the volume remains constant. Since work is defined as W=PΔV, where ΔV=0 means no work is performed. Therefore, no work is done in this process.

questions.text: In an adiabatic process, how does the temperature of a gas change as its volume decreases?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. If volume decreases (compression), the gas molecules collide more frequently, increasing internal energy and raising the temperature. Temperature decreases only when the gas expands, not when it compresses.
During an adiabatic process, when the volume of a gas decreases, its temperature increases due to the work done on the gas, which raises its internal energy

This option is incorrect. A constant temperature process occurs in an isothermal process, not an adiabatic one. In an adiabatic process, the temperature changes because no heat is exchanged, but work is done.
This option is incorrect. The temperature only increases during compression in an adiabatic process. It does not decrease unless the gas later expands.

questions.explanation: During an adiabatic process, when the volume of a gas decreases, its temperature increases due to the work done on the gas, which raises its internal energy.

questions.text: During an isothermal expansion of an ideal gas, which of the following statement is true?
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: Pressure and temperature of the gas increase
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. In an isothermal expansion, temperature remains constant by definition. Pressure decreases as volume increases, according to Boyle’s Law.

options.text: The internal energy of the gas increases
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. The internal energy (UUU) of an ideal gas depends only on temperature. Since temperature remains constant in an isothermal process, internal energy does not change.

options.text: The temperature of the gas remains constant
options.explanation: This option is correct. By definition, an isothermal process means the temperature of the gas does not change. Any heat energy added to the system is completely converted into work done by the gas.

options.text: The work done by the gas is zero
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. In an isothermal expansion, the gas does positive work as it expands. Since Q=WQ = WQ=W, the gas absorbs heat energy and uses it to do work on the surroundings.

questions.explanation: C is the correct option.

questions.text: Consider an ideal gas confined to the cylinder with a fixed piston, on heating the gas, all the heat supplied increases
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is correct. As the gas is heated, the kinetic energy of the gas molecules increases. This increase in kinetic energy is directly related to the increase in the temperature of the gas, as described by the Kinetic Molecular Theory. The kinetic energy is proportional to the temperature of the gas, and heating the gas causes the molecules to move faster.

This option is incorrect. In an ideal gas, the potential energy of the molecules is generally assumed to be zero because ideal gas molecules do not experience intermolecular forces. Heating the gas does not change the potential energy, as the ideal gas model assumes no significant intermolecular interactions.

options.text: The intermolecular forces between gas molecules
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. In an ideal gas, the intermolecular forces are assumed to be negligible. The model assumes that the gas molecules do not interact with each other except during elastic collisions. Heating the gas does not affect intermolecular forces because they are not considered in the ideal gas model.

This option is incorrect. The number of gas molecules remains constant unless gas is added or removed from the system. Heating the gas does not affect the number of molecules in the gas, only their motion (kinetic energy) is increased.

questions.explanation: For an ideal gas, the internal energy (U) is directly related to its temperature. Heating the gas increases its internal energy. The increase in internal energy results in a rise in the temperature of the gas

questions.text: Carnot cycle consists of
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Isothermal processes are constant temperature processes. While part of the Carnot cycle, it's not the entire cycle.Therefore, this is not the full answer.
Adiabatic processes are those with no heat exchange. While part of the Carnot cycle, it's not the entire cycle.Therefore, this is not the full answer.
The Carnot cycle consists of two isothermal and two adiabatic processes.These steps are essential for its maximum efficiency.Therefore, this is the correct answer.
The Carnot cycle is defined by specific thermodynamic processes. It relies on both isothermal and adiabatic steps. Therefore, this is incorrect.

questions.explanation: The Carnot cycle consists of two isothermal and two adiabatic processes.These steps are essential for its maximum efficiency.Therefore, this is the correct answer.

questions.text: If heat equal to 0.1 J is provided to the gas contained in a cylinder and it expands through 0.1m3 at 1N/m2 then its internal energy:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is incorrect. The internal energy remains the same.Therefore, this option is incorrect.
This would happen only if more work was done than heat supplied.Therefore, this option is incorrect.

Since the heat added equals the work done, the internal energy does not change.Therefore, this option is correct. Use the first law of thermodynamics: ΔU = Q - W, where:ΔU = change in internal energyQ = heat added to the system (0.1 J)W = work done by the system (PΔV)W = PΔV = 1 N/m² × 0.1 m³ = 0.1 JΔU = 0.1 J - 0.1 J = 0 J since the change in internal energy is 0 so thats means it remains the same
This would only be true if 0.2J of work was done.Therefore, this option is incorrect.

questions.text: Which of the following condition must be true for transfer of energy from an object at temperature T1​ to another object at temperature T2​?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: If two objects are at the same temperature, no heat transfer occurs because thermal equilibrium has been reached.
Heat flows from a higher-temperature object to a lower-temperature object, so this condition would not result in energy transfer from T1 to T2
Heat transfer occurs from the hotter object to the colder object, as described by the second law of thermodynamics.
Since option (c) is correct, this option cannot be valid.

questions.explanation: Heat transfer occurs from the hotter object to the colder object, as described by the second law of thermodynamics.

questions.text: Find the change in internal energy of the system when a system absorbs 2 kilocalorie of heat and at the same time does 500 joule of work

options.explanation: Correct. Applying the first law of thermodynamics: ΔU = Q - W Q = 2 kcal = 2000 cal = 2000 × 4.186 J = 8372 J W = 500 J ΔU = 8372 J - 500 J = 7872 J ≈ 7900 J
